main_summary.summary: Vernon F. Dahmer Jr., born August 27, 1929 in the Kelly Settlement north of Hattiesburg, Mississippi, was interviewed December 1, 2015 by Emilye Crosby with John Bishop and Guha Shankar at the University of Southern Mississippi Center for Oral History and Cultural Heritage. As eldest son of Vernon F. Dahmer Sr. (NAACP Forrest County branch president), Dahmer Jr. recounts the family's mixed-race genealogy descending from German immigrant Peter Dahmer and the Kelly family, his father's operation of a farm, school bus, sawmill, and grocery store, and his own attendance at Shady Grove Baptist Church school, Tougaloo, and Alcorn State University before being drafted in 1951. He describes his 27-year U.S. Air Force career as a teletype/communications specialist with Top Secret clearance, postings at Sheppard AFB, Francis E. Warren AFB, Tyndall AFB, Shemya in the Aleutians, Germany, and March AFB. He provides primary-source testimony on the January 10, 1966 White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan firebombing that killed his father, his decision to walk into the Riverside FBI office demanding non-Mississippi investigators, the seven Dahmer sons' combined 78 years of military service, and the 1998 conviction of Klan Imperial Wizard Sam Bowers in which he served as primary family spokesperson.
key_themes: Forrest County NAACP and voter registration
main_summary.historical_significance:
Dahmer Jr.'s testimony is a first-person primary source on the 1966 assassination of Vernon Dahmer Sr., one of the most documented Klan murders of a Mississippi civil rights organizer. The interview supplies eyewitness detail unavailable in court records: the family's mixed-race origins in the Kelly Settlement, the father's pre-Brown NAACP activism dating to the mid-1940s, the routine denial of voter registration after poll-tax payment witnessed by Dahmer Jr. as a teenager, and the sons' simultaneous active-duty Air Force service during the very period their father was being targeted by the White Knights.

The account also documents an under-recognized institutional pattern: a Black Mississippi family in which seven of eight siblings served a combined 78 years in the United States military while their father was killed by domestic terrorists his federal government failed to prosecute for 32 years. Dahmer Jr.'s narrative of walking into the Riverside FBI office to demand non-Mississippi investigators, his role as family spokesperson in the 1998 Sam Bowers retrial, and his mother Ellie Dahmer's three terms as Forrest County election commissioner extend the historical record beyond the murder itself into the family's multi-decade pursuit of accountability.

chapters.title: Birth and Family Origins
chapters.summary: Vernon F. Dahmer Jr. introduces himself, born August 27, 1929 in the Kelly Settlement five miles north of Hattiesburg. He describes his father Vernon Dahmer Sr. as the youngest of 12 children born to George Dahmer and Ellen Kelly Dahmer, and explains how segregation drove his father's six sisters and elder brothers to leave Mississippi for the North, where the light-complexioned sons assimilated as white in Chicago.

chapters.title: Mixed-Race Genealogy
chapters.summary: Dahmer traces the family's mixed-race origins to Peter Dahmer, a German immigrant from Darmstadt, and Laura Barnes, his grandfather George Dahmer's mother born out of wedlock and raised by a mulatto woman running a boarding house in Covington County. He describes how Laura Barnes later married former slave Charlie Craft and bore nine mixed-race children, and how the Kelly Settlement community accommodated multiple mixed-race families including the Wilson and Eaton families.

chapters.title: The Family Farm and Businesses
chapters.summary: Dahmer describes his father building a multi-enterprise operation in Forrest County: a several-hundred-acre farm, a school bus he leased to the segregated county school system around 1935, a tractor purchased with Camp Shelby economic growth in 1940, a grocery store opened in 1942, and a sawmill added in 1945. He recounts his own childhood labor delivering 300-pound ice blocks, cutting meat, and working three lines of family income.

chapters.title: Three Mothers and the Family Nucleus
chapters.summary: Dahmer recounts the structure of his father's three marriages: divorce from his biological mother in 1935, marriage to a second wife who died in 1949 leaving three more sons, and marriage to Ellen (Ellie) Dahmer in 1952, who raised the two youngest children Dennis and Betty Dahmer. He describes growing up with a father who was a disciplinarian focal point and the bonded unity among the brothers that persists today.

chapters.title: Shady Grove and Country Schooling
chapters.summary: Dahmer describes the Shady Grove Baptist Church, established 1864 on land donated by his great-grandfather Warren Kelly (descendant of slave master Green Kelly and his African mistress Sarah), and the adjacent one-room cabin that served as the first school. He recounts the segregated curriculum which nonetheless included Negro history (Booker T. Washington, George Washington Carver, the Underground Railroad) through a progressive Black principal, the hand-me-down white-school textbooks, and his father's nightly fireplace spelling drills.

chapters.title: Tougaloo and Alcorn State
chapters.summary: Dahmer recounts his post-1948 college years: enrollment at Tougaloo, where he encountered white faculty under President Harold C. Warren, withdrawal after illness, and transfer to Alcorn State University as an agriculture major under the work-study program. He describes the contrast between Tougaloo's fluid student body and Alcorn's rural country-boy culture, and his work milking and mopping in the campus dairy.

chapters.title: Father's NAACP Activism and Voter Suppression
chapters.summary: Dahmer testifies to witnessing his father attempt to register to vote in Forrest County in the mid-1940s after paying poll tax to the sheriff. He recounts the white registrar handing his father a card and falsely claiming his father misread it, a denial Dahmer Jr. recognized at the time as untrue. He describes his father co-founding the Forrest County NAACP branch with eight other Black men around 1945, holding secret nighttime meetings, and becoming branch president in the mid-1950s after Brown v. Board.

chapters.title: Draft and Air Force Communications Career
chapters.summary: Dahmer recounts being drafted in September 1951 at the height of the Korean War and switching to the Air Force the day before his Army induction on his uncle's advice. He describes basic training at Sheppard Air Force Base, teletype school at Francis E. Warren Air Force Base in Cheyenne, Wyoming, an interrogation by federal investigators over the unanswered draft notice, his failed attempt to transfer to truck-driving school, and his rise through Tyndall AFB Florida, Shemya in the Aleutians, Germany, and ultimately to senior master sergeant.

chapters.title: January 10, 1966 Firebombing
chapters.summary: Dahmer describes receiving the 2-3 AM telephone call on January 10, 1966 from his aunt in Compton, California reporting the White Knights firebombing of his father's home and grocery store. He recounts walking into the FBI office in Riverside the next morning to demand that no Mississippi law enforcement be assigned to the case, the FBI's offended response, and his colonel's prior briefing not to compromise his Top Secret communications position. He describes the airport-to-bus journey home shadowed by a federal observer and the worst day of his life viewing his father's body in the morgue and the destroyed family site.

chapters.title: Country Failed, Family Endured
chapters.summary: Dahmer reflects on the contradiction that seven of his father's sons served a combined 78 years on active duty across the Korean and Vietnam eras while the same federal government failed to protect their father from domestic terrorists. He recounts his stepmother Ellie Dahmer's decision to stay on the property and rebuild rather than relocate to her brothers in Washington, her seven trial appearances, her three terms as Forrest County election commissioner, and her role as family leader.

chapters.title: Sam Bowers 1998 Conviction
chapters.summary: Dahmer describes the 1998 prosecution of Sam Bowers, Imperial Wizard of the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan in Jones County, who had told fellow Klansmen they could kill a Black male without facing jail. He recounts the Medgar Evers case opening the legal pathway, his role as primary family spokesperson with his mother and brother Dennis Dahmer, the August 21, 1998 jury conviction at 2-3 PM, Bowers' death in prison in November 2006, and the 25 years of annual January 10 memorial services at the Hub City Professional Men's Club in Hattiesburg that kept the case alive.
related_events: Medgar Evers case (Byron De La Beckwith 1994)

chapters.title: Photo Documentation
chapters.summary: Dahmer narrates a series of family photographs for the camera: a portrait of his father Vernon F. Dahmer Sr. in his youth, the family home before the firebombing, the burned-out home structure with brothers in military uniform overlooking the site, the grocery store before and after the firebombing, the funeral, and the destroyed family car.
